narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 45.3% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This threshold reflects the school's cautious approach to ensuring incoming students are likely to succeed in the demanding legal field. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles among similar applicants. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, right around the dataset average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, showing solid academic consistency from college. Similarly, your LSAT score of 31, though below the average of 36.70, still contributed positively by demonstrating baseline reasoning skills that help many students pass the bar. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile stands out for that lower Year 3 ranking, which outweighed your other strengths in the model's eyes, even with your full-time commitment aligning with the vast majority (over 92%) of successful applicants. While family background like your upper-middle income didn't sway things much here, these top three academic markers drove the overall decision. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your resilience and any upward trends. Schools value growth stories like yours, and targeted prep could shift that probability dramatically in your favor.
